records.display_name: B&T Open Road Tolling Savings

payload.claim_text: During 2023, concrete initiatives have been identified to generate savings that lower the outyear deficits by $23 million for B&T annually beginning in 2024. The $23 million savings is driven primarily from the reduction of vacant positions due to Open Road Tolling efficiencies ($103 million over the Plan period).
payload.subject: MTA Bridges and Tunnels

records.raw_text: During 2023, concrete initiatives have been identified to generate savings that lower the outyear deficits by $23 million for B&T annually beginning in 2024. The $23 million savings is driven primarily from the reduction of vacant positions due to Open Road Tolling efficiencies ($103 million over the Plan period).

records.display_name: CBDTP Authorization

payload.claim_text: The Central Business District Tolling Program (CBDTP) was authorized by the MTA Reform and Traffic Mobility Act, enacted in April 2019.

records.raw_text: The CBDTP, which was authorized by the MTA Reform and Traffic Mobility Act, enacted in April 2019

records.display_name: B&T Toll Collection Consultant Contracts

payload.claim_text: Three competitively solicited personal service contracts awarded to Atkins, Gannett Fleming, and HDR for miscellaneous toll collection consultant support services on an as-needed basis in the aggregate not-to-exceed amount of $10 million over five years.
payload.subject: MTA Bridges and Tunnels

records.raw_text: Three competitively solicited personal service contracts awarded to Atkins, Gannett Fleming, and HDR for miscellaneous toll collection consultant support services on an as-needed basis in the aggregate not-to-exceed amount of $10 million over five years.

records.display_name: CBD Definition

payload.claim_text: The Central Business District (CBD) is defined as Manhattan south of and inclusive of 60th Street, not including the FDR Drive or the West Side Highway (which includes the Battery Park underpass and or any surface roadway portion of the Hugh L. Carey Tunnel that connects to West Street).

records.raw_text: CBD, defined as Manhattan south of and inclusive of 60th Street, not including the FDR Drive or the West Side Highway (which includes the Battery Park underpass and or any surface roadway portion of the Hugh L. Carey Tunnel that connects to West Street)

Central Business District Tolling Program

description: First of its kind in the U.S., designed to reduce traffic congestion, improve air quality, promote mass transit usage, and raise revenue for key capital projects. Managed by MTA Bridges and Tunnels. Preliminary design of tolling infrastructure advanced in 2021.

Aims to reduce congestion in Manhattan's Central Business District and generate net revenue to support $15 billion for the MTA 2020-2024 Capital Program. Will impose a toll for vehicles entering or remaining in the CBD, defined as Manhattan south of and inclusive of 60th Street, not including the FDR Drive or the West Side Highway.
Aims to reduce congestion in Manhattan's CBD and generate sufficient net revenue to support $15 billion for the MTA 2020-2024 Capital Program.
Program and construction management and inspection services for the Central Business District Tolling Program (congestion pricing)

CBDTP will impose a toll for vehicles entering or remaining in the Central Business District (CBD), defined as Manhattan south of and inclusive of 60th Street, not including the FDR Drive or the West Side Highway. Environmental Assessment concluded in late June 2023 when FHWA issued a Finding of No Significant Impact (FONSI). Contractors have up to 310 days from approval to complete design, development, testing and installation.
Congestion pricing tolling program critical to the Capital Program; largest funding source in the 2020-2024 Capital Program at $15,000 million
Modification to TransCore contract to add $48.5M and extend 40 months for design, build, operate, and maintain CBDTP
Congestion Pricing scheduled for implementation in mid-2024

Aims to reduce congestion in Manhattan's Central Business District (CBD) and generate sufficient net revenue to support $15 billion for the MTA 2020-2024 Capital Program and subsequent capital programs. Authorized by the MTA Reform and Traffic Mobility Act, enacted in April 2019. Will impose a toll for vehicles entering or remaining in the CBD, defined as Manhattan south of and inclusive of 60th Street, not including the FDR Drive or the West Side Highway (which includes the Battery Park underpass and or any surface roadway portion of the Hugh L. Carey Tunnel that connects to West Street).

Aims to reduce congestion in Manhattan's Central Business District (CBD) and generate sufficient net revenue to support $15 billion for the MTA 2020-2024 Capital Program. Defined as Manhattan south of and inclusive of 60th Street, not including the FDR Drive or the West Side Highway.
Infrastructure and Systems for congestion pricing tolling program
Implementing Congestion Pricing
Discussed at MTA Finance Committee meeting; proceeds from TBTA bonds to finance a portion of the capital costs of the Central Business District Tolling Program

The CBDTP implementation has been paused by New York State as of June 5, 2024. The Board extended the implementation date from in or about June 2024 until after execution of the required final tolling agreement among Project Sponsors (TBTA, NYSDOT, NYCDOT) and FHWA.
Board approval of phase-in feature for CBD Charge Schedule: Phase 1 (2025-2027) at 60% of adopted rates, Phase 2 (2028-2030) at 80%, Phase 3 (starting 2031) at full March 2024 adopted rates

Established by State legislation in 2019 to manage traffic congestion in Manhattan and provide $15 billion in capital funding for the MTA 2020-2024 Capital Program. On June 5, 2024, Governor Hochul announced pause of implementation. MTA Board resolution on June 26, 2024 recognized pause, extended implementation until tolling agreement executed.
Congestion pricing financial results presentation covering revenue, expenses, and debt issuance for system infrastructure costs and initial capital projects
Congestion pricing program tolls vehicles entering the Congestion Relief Zone in Manhattan, launched by MTA Bridges and Tunnels.
Congestion pricing program collecting tolls in the Congestion Relief Zone
Tolling program for the Congestion Relief Zone in Manhattan
Authorization of the TBTA Congestion Relief Zone Toll Revenue Obligation Resolution for Submission to the Capital Program Review Board

CRZ Tolling Program established pursuant to the Traffic Mobility Act. Requires TBTA to establish and operate the CRZ Tolling Program and charge variable tolls for vehicles entering the Congestion Relief Zone. Program went into effect on January 5, 2025. Required to generate net annual revenues over time to fund $15 billion for MTA transit and commuter rail system capital projects.

The MTA Finance Department is seeking TBTA Board ratification of the CRZ Toll Revenue Obligation Resolution, including Master Glossary of Terms and Standard Resolution Provisions, to enable TBTA to issue bonds and notes to finance approved Capital Program transit and commuter projects.
Enables TBTA to issue bonds and notes secured by CRZ revenues to fund $15 billion portion of the 2020-2024 Capital Program for key transit and commuter rail capital projects
CBDTP November Financial Plan - 2025 November Forecast Accrual Statement of Operations
February Financial Plan - 2025 Adopted Budget Accrual Statement of Operations
February Financial Plan - 2025 Adopted Budget, reporting March 2026 actuals

MTA Finance Department seeking TBTA Board authorization for issuance of up to $506 million in bonds/notes to fund design, build, and installation of infrastructure, tolling systems and allowable implementation expenses for CBDTP
Congestion pricing program with capital commitments at B&T. 17 commitments totaling $45.2 million in 2020.
Program to toll vehicles entering or remaining in Manhattan's Central Business District to reduce congestion and raise revenue for MTA's 2020-2024 Capital Program

Board approved TBTA supplemental resolutions to issue TBTA CBDTP Second Subordinate Revenue Obligations up to $506 million to fund design, build, and installation of infrastructure, tolling systems and implementation expenses for the CBDTP.
TBTA Board authorized up to $506M in CBDTP Second Subordinate Revenue Obligations to fund design, build, and installation of infrastructure, tolling systems, and allowable implementation expenses.
February Financial Plan for 2026-2029 with 2025 Final Estimate and 2026 Adopted Budget
Advance Central Business District Tolling. Announced public meeting schedule. Working with external partners.
Environmental Assessment (EA) Process Overview

Program to reduce congestion in Manhattan's Central Business District via electronic tolling; authorized by MTA Reform and Traffic Mobility Act, enacted April 2019. Includes installation of electronic tolling equipment on the perimeter and within the CBD, defined as streets south and inclusive of 60th Street in Manhattan, excluding FDR Drive, NYS Route 9A/West Side Highway, Battery Park Underpass, and surface road portion of Hugh L Carey Tunnel. Environmental Assessment (EA) underway per FHWA guidance. FHWA decision anticipated in late 2022.
Aims to reduce congestion in Manhattan's Central Business District and generate revenue to support $15 billion in bonding capacity for MTA Capital Programs
CBD Tolling Program under TBTA Operations; 41 budgeted positions, 7 actual, 34 vacant
CBDTP Environmental Assessment and tolling program progress update as of July 2022
Aims to reduce congestion in Manhattan's Central Business District (CBD) and generate $15 billion in revenue to support the MTA 2020 – 2024 Capital Program and subsequent capital programs
The nation's first Congestion Pricing Program, implemented by TBTA. $503 million budget to reduce congestion in Manhattan's Central Business District.

Additional funding of $8.725 million for WSP USA to complete the environmental documentation for the Central Business District Tolling Program Environmental review, as required by the Federal Highway Administration under the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA). Includes finalizing the Environmental Assessment, preparing outreach materials, and developing final documentation.
